does: This paper systematizes the security and safety risks within the Model Context Protocol ecosystem through a comprehensive taxonomy that distinguishes between adversarial security threats and epistemic safety hazards. The work analyzes structural vulnerabilities in MCP's core primitives—Resources, Prompts, and Tools—and demonstrates how context can be weaponized to trigger unauthorized operations in multi-agent environments. The authors categorize specific attack vectors including indirect prompt injection and tool poisoning, while examining how the decoupling of context from execution creates novel threat surfaces. The paper surveys existing defensive approaches such as cryptographic provenance verification through techniques like ETDI and runtime intent verification mechanisms, establishing a foundational framework for understanding the risk landscape introduced by MCP's emergence as a standard protocol for connecting LLMs to external systems and data sources.
connects: This work builds on earlier research in LLM security vulnerabilities, particularly prompt injection attacks and their variants, while extending threat modeling from single-agent systems to distributed multi-agent environments enabled by standardized protocols. It connects to prior work on AI alignment and epistemic safety in language models, distinguishing these concerns from traditional cybersecurity threats. The paper engages with research on tool-using AI systems and the risks of autonomous agent action, building on previous explorations of how LLMs interact with external APIs and databases. The framing of MCP as analogous to USB-C situates this work within broader discussions of AI interoperability standards and their security implications. The defense mechanisms surveyed draw from cryptographic verification literature and runtime monitoring approaches previously applied to other distributed systems.
enables: This systematization lays groundwork for developing MCP-specific security standards and best practices as the protocol becomes more widely adopted across AI applications. The taxonomy enables future research into novel defense mechanisms that can operate at the protocol level to prevent context weaponization in multi-agent scenarios. The work points toward new research directions in intent verification for distributed tool delegation, cryptographic approaches to ensuring tool provenance, and formal methods for analyzing context-execution decoupling. It establishes a foundation for building secure MCP implementations and informs the design of safer protocol extensions. The framework also enables evaluation of emerging AI agents against a structured threat model, supporting the development of security certification mechanisms for MCP-compatible tools and prompting principled architectural choices in systems that rely on standardized agent-to-tool interfaces.
